content.text: What is Unit Testing

A unit test verifies the behaviour of the smallest testable piece of code — a single function, method, or class — in complete isolation from all external dependencies. Databases, HTTP APIs, file systems, clocks, and third-party services are replaced with controlled substitutes (mocks or fakes) so that the test exercises only the logic under examination. The term "unit" refers to the unit of composition: the smallest piece of code that can be understood and verified independently.

In Python, unit tests are most commonly written with pytest, which provides a clean, minimal API for defining test functions, fixtures, and parametrised cases. A unit test suite is the first and fastest layer of the test pyramid — it should complete in seconds and provide immediate feedback after every code change.
Why It Matters

Unit tests are the fastest safety net in software development. Because they make no I/O calls and spawn no subprocesses, a suite of thousands of unit tests can complete in under ten seconds on a modern laptop. This speed makes it practical to run them after every change — before committing, before pushing, and in CI on every pull request. The cost of a failing unit test is seconds of feedback time; the cost of a production bug discovered by a user is orders of magnitude higher.

For AI agent development specifically, unit tests are where you verify the deterministic, algorithmic parts of the system: prompt construction functions that assemble context from retrieved documents, tool output parsers that extract structured data from LLM responses, retry and backoff logic, cost budget enforcement, and the validation functions that check that tool arguments conform to their schemas. These functions are entirely deterministic and should be covered exhaustively with unit tests before you ever call a real LLM.

attrs.alt: Unit test isolation: single function under test, all external deps mocked; AAA pattern: Arrange → Act → Assert

content.text: Benefits
Extremely fast: unit tests run in milliseconds; a suite of 1,000 tests completes in a few seconds.
Precise failure localisation: a failing unit test points directly to the function that broke, not to a system interaction.
Enables safe refactoring: green unit tests prove that a refactor preserves all specified behaviour.
Documents behaviour: well-named tests serve as executable documentation of what each function is supposed to do.
Cheap to run: no infrastructure needed; runs on any developer machine without Docker or cloud credentials.
Enables TDD: the fast feedback loop makes it practical to write tests before implementation.
Reduces debugging time: bugs are caught at the source, not after propagating through multiple layers.
Trade-offs
Cannot detect integration bugs: a function that passes all unit tests may still fail when connected to a real database or HTTP service.
Requires mocking discipline: poorly written mocks give false confidence without testing real behaviour.
High coverage does not equal high quality: 100% line coverage with bad tests is worse than 70% coverage with excellent tests.
Maintenance overhead: tests must be updated when the code they test changes; outdated tests are worse than no tests.
Unit isolation can be artificial: splitting highly coupled code into isolated units requires upfront design work.
How Unit Tests Work

A unit test follows the Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern. In the Arrange phase, you set up the inputs, fixtures, and mocked dependencies that the code under test will receive. In the Act phase, you call the function under test exactly once. In the Assert phase, you verify the return value, or the interactions with mocked dependencies, or both. Each test should test one thing and have a single reason to fail.

pytest test functions are discovered automatically: any function whose name starts with test_ in a file whose name starts with test_ or ends with _test.py is run as a test. Fixtures are functions decorated with @pytest.fixture that are injected by name into test functions. This dependency injection is how shared state (test data, mock objects, temporary directories) is provided to tests without polluting global scope.
The AAA Pattern
Arrange
— create the inputs, instantiate the SUT, configure mock return values.
Act
— call the function or method under test. One call per test.
Assert
— verify the return value, or raised exception, or mock interactions.
Architecture

Unit tests live alongside the production code they test. A common convention is to mirror the source tree under a tests/ directory: tests/test_calculator.py tests src/calculator.py. Another convention (used in some Python projects) co-locates tests in the same package as the source, using a tests/ subdirectory within each package. Either works; the key is consistency.
Test Discovery and Naming

pytest discovers tests automatically based on naming conventions. Test files must be named test_*.py or *_test.py. Test functions and methods must be named test_*. Test classes (for grouping related tests) must be named Test*. Following these conventions means you never need to register tests manually.
When to Use Unit Tests
Any pure function: one with no side effects, whose output depends only on its inputs.
Business logic: discount calculations, validation rules, state machine transitions.
Parsers and formatters: functions that transform one data structure to another.
Algorithm implementations: sorting, search, chunking, tokenisation.
Error handling: verify that the right exception is raised with the right message for invalid inputs.
Edge cases: empty inputs, None values, boundary values, very large inputs, Unicode strings.
Agent helper functions: prompt builders, response parsers, tool argument validators.
All utility modules: date formatting, string manipulation, configuration parsing.
How to Implement

The following examples cover the most important pytest features for unit testing: basic assertions, parametrize for data-driven tests, fixtures for shared setup, testing exceptions, and testing side effects via mocks.
Step 1: Basic test functions with assertions
Step 2: Parametrize for data-driven tests

# tests/test_calculator.py
import pytest
from myapp.calculator import add, divide, is_prime

# Test multiple input/output pairs with one test definition
@pytest.mark.parametrize("a, b, expected", [
    (2, 3, 5),
    (-1, 1, 0),
    (0, 0, 0),
    (100, -50, 50),
    (0.1, 0.2, pytest.approx(0.3)),
])
def test_add(a, b, expected):
    assert add(a, b) == expected

@pytest.mark.parametrize("n, expected", [
    (2, True),
    (3, True),
    (4, False),
    (17, True),
    (100, False),
    (1, False),
    (0, False),
])
def test_is_prime(n, expected):
    assert is_prime(n) == expected

# Parametrize exception tests
@pytest.mark.parametrize("a, b", [
    (10, 0),
    (-5, 0),
])
def test_divide_raises_zero_division_error(a, b):
    with pytest.raises(ZeroDivisionError):
        divide(a, b)
Step 3: Fixtures for shared test data and setup
Step 4: Testing side effects and return values together

# tests/test_order_processor.py
import pytest
from unittest.mock import MagicMock, patch, call

def test_process_order_calls_payment_and_inventory():
    """Verify that processing an order charges payment AND decrements inventory."""
    mock_payment = MagicMock()
    mock_inventory = MagicMock()
    mock_payment.charge.return_value = {"transaction_id": "txn_123"}
    mock_inventory.decrement.return_value = True

    from myapp.order_processor import process_order
    result = process_order(
        payment_service=mock_payment,
        inventory_service=mock_inventory,
        customer_id="cust_1",
        sku="SKU-001",
        quantity=2,
        unit_price=25.00,
    )

    # Assert return value
    assert result["status"] == "confirmed"
    assert result["transaction_id"] == "txn_123"

    # Assert side effects: payment charged with correct amount
    mock_payment.charge.assert_called_once_with(
        customer_id="cust_1",
        amount=50.00,  # 2 * 25.00
    )

    # Assert side effects: inventory decremented
    mock_inventory.decrement.assert_called_once_with(sku="SKU-001", quantity=2)

def test_process_order_does_not_charge_if_inventory_unavailable():
    mock_payment = MagicMock()
    mock_inventory = MagicMock()
    mock_inventory.decrement.return_value = False  # out of stock

    from myapp.order_processor import process_order
    with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="SKU-001 is out of stock"):
        process_order(
            payment_service=mock_payment,
            inventory_service=mock_inventory,
            customer_id="cust_1",
            sku="SKU-001",
            quantity=5,
            unit_price=25.00,
        )

    # Critical: payment must NOT have been charged when inventory check fails
    mock_payment.charge.assert_not_called()
Examples: Testing Agent Helper Functions

# tests/test_prompt_builder.py
# Unit tests for a prompt construction function used in an AI agent
import pytest
from myapp.agent.prompt_builder import build_retrieval_prompt, build_tool_result_message

def test_build_retrieval_prompt_includes_all_documents():
    docs = [
        {"id": "doc1", "content": "Python is a language.", "score": 0.95},
        {"id": "doc2", "content": "pytest is a framework.", "score": 0.88},
    ]
    query = "What is Python?"
    prompt = build_retrieval_prompt(query=query, documents=docs)

    assert "Python is a language." in prompt
    assert "pytest is a framework." in prompt
    assert "What is Python?" in prompt

def test_build_retrieval_prompt_respects_max_tokens():
    docs = [{"id": f"doc{i}", "content": "x" * 500, "score": 0.9} for i in range(10)]
    prompt = build_retrieval_prompt(query="test", documents=docs, max_tokens=200)
    # Rough check: prompt should be truncated
    assert len(prompt.split()) < 300

def test_build_retrieval_prompt_empty_documents():
    prompt = build_retrieval_prompt(query="test", documents=[])
    assert "No relevant documents" in prompt or "context" in prompt.lower()

def test_build_tool_result_message_structures_output():
    result = build_tool_result_message(
        tool_use_id="toolu_01",
        content='{"status": "ok", "data": [1, 2, 3]}',
    )
    assert result["type"] == "tool_result"
    assert result["tool_use_id"] == "toolu_01"
    assert result["content"] == '{"status": "ok", "data": [1, 2, 3]}'

@pytest.mark.parametrize("bad_id", ["", None, "   "])
def test_build_tool_result_message_raises_on_bad_id(bad_id):
    with pytest.raises((ValueError, TypeError)):
        build_tool_result_message(tool_use_id=bad_id, content="ok")
Real-World Example

A team building an AI research assistant has a prompt_builder module responsible for assembling the system prompt, retrieved document snippets, and tool definitions into a message list. This logic is entirely deterministic: given the same inputs, it always produces the same output. The team writes 35 unit tests covering normal operation, edge cases (no documents, very long documents, documents with special characters), and error conditions (invalid tool schemas, missing required fields). These tests run in 0.3 seconds and catch every regression in prompt construction. The LLM itself is never called in these tests — only the code that prepares its inputs.
Common Mistakes
Testing the implementation rather than the behaviour: if you refactor the code and the test breaks even though the observable behaviour is unchanged, the test was wrong.
Multiple assertions testing multiple behaviours in a single test — makes failures hard to diagnose.
Using hard-coded magic values without comments explaining their significance.
Over-mocking: mocking internal functions within the same module couples the test to the implementation detail.
Asserting on mock.called without asserting on the arguments — misses argument-level regressions.
Ignoring return values: if a function returns something, assert on it.
Writing tests that always pass: a test that never fails does not protect against regressions.
Skipping edge cases: the most important tests are often for None, empty string, zero, negative numbers, and boundary values.
Best Practices
Name tests as sentences: test_charge_user_raises_value_error_when_balance_is_zero is better than test_charge_3.
One assertion per test where possible — or one logical concept per test.
Use pytest.approx() for floating-point comparisons, never ==.
Use parametrize to cover many input cases without duplicating test code.
Keep fixtures small and focused; compose them rather than making one large fixture.
Put shared fixtures in conftest.py at the appropriate directory level.
Use tmp_path (a built-in pytest fixture) for any test that needs to create real files.
Run pytest with -v (verbose) during development for readable output; use --tb=short in CI.
Aim for meaningful coverage — cover all branches, not just all lines.
Performance and Scalability

A well-maintained unit test suite scales to thousands of tests with sub-second runtimes per test. The key is strict isolation: no network calls, no disk I/O, no sleep. If a test takes more than 100ms, it is probably doing I/O that should be mocked. Use pytest --durations=10 to identify the slowest tests and investigate them. For suites with thousands of tests, pytest-xdist enables parallel execution across CPU cores with a single flag: pytest -n auto.
Tooling and Ecosystem
pytest
— test runner, fixture system, parametrize, plugins. The standard for Python.
pytest-cov
— code coverage measurement; integrates with coverage.py.
pytest-xdist
— parallel test execution: -n auto uses all CPU cores.
pytest-watch (ptw)
— file watcher that reruns tests on save; essential for TDD.
hypothesis
— property-based testing: generates random inputs to find edge cases automatically.
faker
— generates realistic fake data (names, emails, addresses) for test fixtures.
factory-boy
— class-based test object factories, integrates with SQLAlchemy models.
pytest-randomly
— randomises test execution order to catch order-dependent failures.
mutmut
— mutation testing: validates your test suite actually catches bugs.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach

Business logic, algorithms, helpers

Formatters, serialisers, UI
Security and Reliability

Unit tests should cover security-sensitive code paths that involve input validation, access control logic, and data sanitisation. A function that validates an API key, sanitises a user-provided filename, or checks that a requested resource belongs to the authenticated user should have unit tests for both valid and invalid inputs. These tests are cheap to write and prevent a class of vulnerabilities that integration tests often miss because they test the happy path.

Do not include real credentials, API keys, or PII in test fixtures. Use obviously fake values (api_key='test_key_000', email='test@example.com') and inject real values from environment variables in integration tests only.
Mental Model

Think of each unit test as a contract clause between the caller and the function. The test says: if you call this function with these inputs, it will return this output and raise this exception under these conditions. The function exists to honour that contract. When the contract changes — because the requirements change — you update the test first, watch it fail, then update the implementation to make it pass again. The test is the spec; the implementation is the fulfilment of the spec.
Key Takeaways
Unit tests verify a single function in complete isolation — all external dependencies are mocked.
Follow the Arrange-Act-Assert pattern: one setup, one call, one assertion per logical behaviour.
Use pytest.mark.parametrize to test many input cases without duplicating test code.
Use pytest fixtures in conftest.py to share reusable test data and mock objects across tests.
Name tests as sentences describing the expected behaviour, not the implementation.
Test edge cases aggressively: None, empty, zero, negative, boundary values, Unicode.
Unit tests are fast and cheap — aim for many of them and run them after every code change.
High line coverage does not guarantee quality; prioritise branch coverage and meaningful assertions.
